title: FloodTraces
text:
Welcome
This website hosts the materials for the FloodTraces policy workshop and hackathon:
Led by the Geographic Data Science Lab at the University of Liverpool, in partnership with the Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) and the International Organization for Migration’s Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM).
FloodTraces develops a replicable data pipeline combining GPS mobility data, social media signals, satellite imagery, and traditional survey sources to produce timely, spatially detailed estimates of flood-driven population displacement. The pipeline generates Digital Traces Situation Reports (SitReps) — concise, policy-oriented documents designed for humanitarian decision-makers in the critical first weeks after a disaster.

title: FloodTraces
text:
Contact

Dr Elisabetta Pietrostefani — e.pietrostefani [at] liverpool.ac.uk
Lecturer in Geographic Data Science, University of Liverpool


Dr Martina Pardy — m.l.pardy [at] liverpool.ac.uk


Matt Mason — matt.mason [at] liverpool.ac.uk

The website is licensed under the Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International.

title: Overview
text:
Case Studies
The global challenge of internal displacement, exacerbated by climate-induced natural hazards, demands more timely and granular data than traditional survey methods alone can provide. In 2024, floods affected an estimated 40 million people worldwide — making them the most common disaster type globally — yet decision-makers in the critical first days after an event often face a near-total absence of reliable displacement figures.
FloodTraces addresses this gap by developing a replicable data pipeline that integrates digital trace data — GPS mobility records, social media signals, satellite imagery, and Cloudflare connectivity estimates — with traditional sources such as IOM’s Displacement Tracking Matrix surveys. The goal is not to replace established methods, but to triangulate and augment them: producing estimates that are faster, spatially finer, and available earlier than field surveys alone can deliver.
The project makes three complementary contributions:
Timely insights when traditional data are scarce.
A replicable pipeline that delivers actionable displacement information in the critical first weeks after a flood, before survey operations are fully operational.
Triangulation and validation of traditional sources.
Cross-referencing GPS signals and other digital traces with IOM DTM data to strengthen the reliability and coverage of displacement assessments.
Anticipatory action in recurrent flood contexts.
Spatially and temporally granular data to inform preparedness in areas with a known history of repeated flooding.
As a proof of concept, the pipeline is applied to three flood events:
Each case study produces a Digital Traces Situation Report (SitRep) — a concise, policy-oriented document showing displacement estimates derived from the GPS pipeline, key validation against OCHA and IOM benchmarks, and a brief summary of data limitations. The Colombia and Indonesia SitReps are presented at the Day 1 policy workshop; the Pakistan case forms the basis for the hackathon.

title: Overview
text:
Partners
This work is led by the Geographic Data Science Lab at the University of Liverpool, and supported by Imago: Data Service for Imager conducted in close partnership with:

FCDO (Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office) — primary funder and policy partner
IOM Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) — operational data partner and validation benchmark

title: Workshop
text:
Schedule at a Glance
22–24 June 2026 · London School of Economics, London UK
The event runs across three days, combining a senior policy workshop on Day 1 with a two-day hackathon on Days 2–3.
Day 1 — Policy Workshop is a high-level dialogue with representatives from FCDO, IOM Displacement Tracking Matrix, NGOs, and partner organisations. The day centres on presenting the Colombia and Indonesia Digital Traces Situation Reports, gathering structured feedback on their usefulness and adoption potential, and surfacing the wider data landscape for flood response. Attendance on Day 1 is required for all hackathon participants — the morning and early afternoon provide essential framing, methodological context, and direct exposure to the policy stakeholders whose feedback shapes hackathon outputs.
Days 2–3 — Hackathon teams work on one of three problem statements using real digital trace mobility, IOM DTM data and contextual data. Selected policy attendees return on Day 3 afternoon for final presentations.

title: Hackathon Problem Statements
text:
Participation
Over two days, your team will tackle one of three challenges using real digital trace data from multiple years of flooding in Pakistan — one of the countries most severely affected by climate-related disasters globally. Your work has the potential to directly inform how IOM, FCDO, and other humanitarian partners think about displacement tracking in future flood events.
Each team selects one problem statement. Your chosen area must be confirmed at the pre-hackathon meeting during the week of 15 June 2026.
All teams are required to use at least one of the provided datasets. The Pakistan case study and full data documentation are described in the following sections — read these before confirming your problem statement.
Participation is structured around pre-assigned mixed teams. Each team includes at least one domain expert (humanitarian or policy), three technical participants (data or code), and one academic contributor. Effective mixing is deliberate — the strongest hackathon outputs combine operational knowledge with analytical capability.

title: Hackathon Problem Statements
text:
1. Replicate and Refine
Take the Colombia and Indonesia Digital Traces SitReps presented on Day 1 and adapt the methodology for the Pakistan case.
The existing reports provide a strong analytical foundation — your team’s task is to bring fresh thinking on visualisations, validation approaches, or how the displacement story is communicated to a policy audience.

How do the GPS displacement signals compare with IOM DTM figures for Pakistan?
Where does the pipeline perform well, and where does it struggle relative to the Colombia/Indonesia benchmarks?
What refinements to the methodology improve the SitRep’s usefulness for operational decision-making?

title: Hackathon Problem Statements
text:
2. Make It Interactive
Turn a static Situation Report into something humanitarian responders can actively explore.
Build an interactive map or analytical platform where users can drill into displacement data dynamically — filtering by geography, time window, or severity. The best builds will be linked from future SitReps via QR code, meaning your work could end up in the hands of decision-makers during the next flood.

What does an interactive SitRep look like in practice for a field responder with 10 minutes?
How do you communicate uncertainty and data limitations in an interactive format without overwhelming the user?
What interaction patterns best serve a non-technical policy audience?

title: Hackathon Problem Statements
text:
3. See It Coming
Move from response to anticipation. Use historical Pakistan flood data to develop analysis or tooling that helps agencies act before displacement peaks.
This is the frontier of humanitarian data work — there is no settled answer, and your team has genuine latitude to explore.

Can GPS mobility signals in the days before a flood peak serve as early warning indicators?
What vulnerability factors (infrastructure, demographics, flood history) best predict where displacement will be most severe?
How can anticipatory outputs be structured so that they are actionable within an agency’s existing operational workflow?








Note



Outputs and recognition
All participant contributions incorporated into future published works will be acknowledged. Selected policy stakeholders will return on Day 3 afternoon for final team presentations. Judges include IOM operational staff, FCDO policy advisors, and academic reviewers.

title: Case-study
text:
Pakistan – Flood Response Community Needs Identification (CNI)
The hackathon draws on a single case study: the 2022 Pakistan floods. Understanding this context is essential background for all three problem statements.
The specific case study for this Hackathon will be the floods that struck Pakistan in 2022. Between June and October of that year, unprecedented monsoon rains affected over 33 million people across the country, resulting in 1,739 deaths and an estimated $40 billion in property damage.
This event was selected for its large-scale humanitarian and economic impact, and the availability of high-quality data from both the International Organization for Migration (IOM) and Meta. These datasets provide an opportunity for participants to compare, combine, and analyse real-world information in a meaningful way.
Focusing on a disaster aligns with the Hackathon’s broader goal: addressing challenges that are becoming increasingly frequent due to climate change. Floods, in particular, represent a recurring threat in Pakistan, making this case both historically relevant and urgently contemporary and is one of the types of disaster events recording the greatest frequency worldwide.
An example of the locations assessed by IOM in Round 6 – Balochistan and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Provinces (August 2024).

title: Data
text:
1. Data Access
This section of the webpage briefly outlines the datasets that should be used during the Hackathon and provides related resources and documentation. The Hackathon’s problem statements can be found here and should be referred to regarding the event’s purpose and objectives.
The human mobility datasets provided for the Hackathon fall into one of two categories: digital data (from Meta) or key informant survey data (from IOM). The table below outlines these, summarising their accessibility, processing and stability. In addition, contextual data on Pakistan is provided to help with analysis, as well as a variety of spatial boundaries that can be used to map the provided datasets. Each dataset is outlined below, alongside any available documentation and guidance for analysis.
All the data mentioned in this document has been uploaded onto Snowflake. As part of the Hackathon, Snowflake colleagues will provide a tutorial on how to access and analyse the data in Snowflake. They will be available throughout the event to answer any queries and a series of guides on using Snowflake can be found on this section of the webpage.

title: Data
text:
2. Datasets

2.1. Facebook Population and Movement During Crisis

Meta provides these data as part of their Data for Good programme in the aftermath of humanitarian disasters. Once uploaded, these data are available to researchers and policymakers for 90 days before being removed from the Data for Good platform. The data shows the number of Facebook users located within a given spatial unit at a given time.
For the Hackathon, we are using the data available in the immediate aftermath of the 2022 Pakistan floods. The data refer to the period 14 August 2022 – 7 September 2022 and are at two geographic scales: 800m tiles (known as quadkeys, based on the Bing Maps tile system) and aggregated to global administrative (GADM) level 2 geographies.
These data consist of two datasets, Population During Crisis and Movement During Crisis:

Population – these are population stock data, showing the number of users in each spatial unit at three snapshots: 00:00, 8:00 and 16:00 (Pacific Time). The data are removed when there are fewer than 10 observations.

Snowflake Schema: META_DATA_FOR_GOOD
Snowflake Views: ADMIN_L2_AGGREGATED_POPULATION and TILE_POPULATION

Movement – these are population flow data, showing the origin and destination of users between temporal points. Users’ origin and destination are chosen according to where they spent most time within each 8-hour window. For example, data recorded at 16:00 shows the flow between areas from 08:00 to 16:00. Where there are fewer than 10 observations for a flow, data are removed. The Pakistan data available for the Movement data are only at the 08:00 and 16:00 time periods, but not for the 00:00 period.

Snowflake Schema: META_DATA_FOR_GOOD
Snowflake Views: ADMIN_L2_AGGREGATED_MOVEMENT and TILE_MOVEMENT


The data are generally comprehensive, but there are some gaps, largely due to collection issues at Meta’s end. For example, in both the tiled and aggregated Movement data, only the 08:00 and 16:00 time stamps are available, with the 00:00 period missing. Additionally, the aggregated Population data for 20, 21 and 22 August are also missing.
Both datasets contain data from a baseline period before the disaster event to compare users’ stock or flow during the crisis. The raw and percentage differences are provided within the dataset, along with a z-score to assess the statistical significance of the change from the pre-crisis baseline to the crisis period.
The data is available as a series of csv files. Each file is either the tiled or aggregated data for each time stamp of each day of the Population or Movement data.

A guide on using these data in R can be found here.


2.2. IOM Data

These data are collated and published by the IOM’s Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) team. The primary data produced for Pakistan by the IOM in the context of the 2022 floods are the Flood Response Community Needs Identification (CNI) datasets. These data are collated and published in rounds; six rounds have been run so far, though data for Round 5 is not publicly available.
The data for all 5 publicly available rounds can be found in Snowflake Schema: IOM_CNI_DATA
Data from the CNI is derived from key informant interviews and direct observations. It provides estimates for the number of temporarily displaced persons (TDPs), the number of returnees and other variables related to displaced populations for each village surveyed. A more detailed methodology for these data can be found in the reports below. These data are often very challenging to collect, and, as a result, each round does not cover the exact same areas, or collect the same level of data.
The data are provided at the village/settlement level. Also included are variables for province, district and Tehsil. For the first two, codes are provided also to make them easier to be joined to spatial boundaries. These codes are p-codes, a geographic framework commonly used by the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) to link data to administrative areas. These geographies and codes do not map onto those that the Facebook Population and Movement data are aggregated to. A short guide on the different geographies is provided at the end of this guidance page.
For the Hackathon, each data round is provided. The data format and variable names vary slightly between rounds, though each generally provides similar variables and data. Alongside each round of data, a report is also published. Reports can be found here for each round:

Round 1 (Nov – Dec 2022)
Round 2 (Jan – Mar 2023)
Round 3 – Balochistan Province (May – June 2023)
Round 3 – Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Province (May – June 2023)
Round 3 – Sindh Province (May – June 2023)
Round 4 – Balochistan Province (August 2023)
Round 4 – Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Province (August 2023)
Round 4 – Sindh Province (August 2023)
Round 6 – Balochistan and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Provinces (August 2024)




2.3. Contextual Data

Alongside mobility data, we have prepared a series of datasets that provide additional context to Pakistan. They consist of population data in raster and aggregated form, and socioeconomic data, also in raster and aggregated format.
Raster population data:

Population estimates for Pakistan for 2020 from WorldPop. The data is at 100m resolution grids in a single raster file.
Population estimates for Pakistan by age and sex for 2020 from WorldPop. The data is at 100m resolution grids in a series of rasters. Files are structured like {iso} {gender} {age group} {year} {type} {resolution}.tif - gender fields are f (female), m (male), t (total); age group fields are 00 (0-12 months), 01 (1-4 years), 05 (5-9 years) and so on until 90 (age 90 and above).

Snowflake Schema: CONTEXTUAL_DATA
Snowflake View: POPULATION_BY_SEX_BY_AGE_GROUP_100MTR_RESOLUTION


Aggregated population data:

Population estimates for Pakistan for 2020 from the WorldPop raster, aggregated (total_pop) to global administrative level 2, and the province and district polygons used by OCHA. The data is calculated by the project team using WorldPop data and spatial polygons provided by GADM and OCHA.

Snowflake Schema: CONTEXTUAL_DATA
Snowflake Views: POPULATION_PAKISTAN_2020_*


Socioeconomic data:

Deprivation data from The Global Gridded Relative Deprivation Index (GRDI). The data is a raster file at 1km resolution, cropped to Pakistan. Data are on a 0-100 scale, with high values indicating higher relative levels of deprivation. Complete documentation for the data can be accessed here. These have also been aggregated to global administrative level 2 and the province and district polygons used by OCHA by computing the mean value (mean_rdi) within the polygon from the GRDI raster data.

Snowflake Schema: CONTEXTUAL_DATA
Snowflake Views: GRDI_*


Climate data:

Satellite detected water extents in Pakistan between 01 and 29 August 2022. The data is a series of shapefiles showing flood extent based on satellite imagery. Data is from the UN Operational Satellite Applications Programme (UNOSAT), found here.
Sentinel-1 based analysis of Pakistan floods, capturing flood progression between 10 August and 23 September 2022. Data is sourced from TU Wein and is compromised of two raster layers at 20-metre resolution: first_flood_detection.tif, the day the flood was first detected, recorded as day-of-year (DOY), and flood_frequency.tif, the percentage of valid observations in which a flood was detected (a proxy for flood permanence).
Dekadal (10-day) rainfall data from 2021 onward at the district level (coded to OCHA administrative level 2) sourced from the World Food Programme (WFP). Data shows series of metrics for rainfall, listed here.
Dekadal (10-day) Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) data from 2021 onward at the district level (coded to OCHA administrative level 2) sourced from the World Food Programme (WFP). Data shows series of metrics for vegetation greenness, listed here, normally used to quantify the health and density of vegetation.
Snowflake Data Access:

Snowflake Schema: CONTEXTUAL_DATA
Snowflake Views: CLIMATE_PAKISTAN_*





2.4. Spatial Boundaries

Due to recent boundary changes and differing administrative boundaries being used by different organisations, the joining of spatial data together for Pakistan is not a simple task. Much of the data listed above, however, is able to be joined to a spatial boundary to be mapped and analysed. This section describes the spatial boundaries made available, as well a table with details of how the Meta, IOM and contextual datasets can be joined to these.
The boundaries available are:

Spatial polygons for global administrative levels, sourced from GADM. The shapefile boundaries for global administrative levels 0, 1, 2 and 3 and the geopackage for Pakistan are included. These boundaries can be joined to the aggregated Facebook Population and Movement data, as well as the population and deprivation data aggregated to global administrative level 2.

Snowflake Schema: SPATIAL_BOUNDARIES
Snowflake Views: PAKISTAN_GADM_ADMIN_LEVEL_POLYGONS

Spatial polygons used by OCHA for subnational boundaries of Pakistan. Data is shapefiles for provinces (adm1), districts (adm2) and tehsils (adm3), sourced from OCHA. These boundaries can be joined to the spatial variable codes in the IOM CNI data for provinces and districts, as well as the population and deprivation data aggregated to OCHA administrative level 1 and 2, and to the WFP climate data.

Snowflake Schema: SPATIAL_BOUNDARIES
Snowflake Views: PAKISTAN_OCHA_ADMIN_LEVEL_POLYGONS

Spatial polygons for the 800m tiles (also known as quadkeys) found in the Facebook Population and Movement tile data. These were created from the tile datasets using the R package quadkeyr.

Snowflake Schema: SPATIAL_BOUNDARIES
Snowflake Views: META_QUADKEY_POLYGONS


The below table provides an overview of the variables in each dataset and which boundary they relate to.

title: Outputs
text:
Pre-Hackathon Meeting
One online pre-hackathon meeting will take place on Thursday 18 or Friday 19 June 2026. The exact date will be confirmed with participants in advance.
This session covers:

Team Formation & Problem Selection: Participants meet their teammates and choose a problem statement to work on.

Gain access to the datasets

title: Output
section: What Groups should keep in mind
text:
What Groups should keep in mind
Participants will focus on triangulating key indicators of displacement and presenting their findings in clear, accessible formats that can inform both policymakers and humanitarian responders. The goal is to develop a compelling “Message + Infographic (Map or Other)”—a concise narrative supported by data visualisation —that can serve as a proof of concept for improving disaster response in future scenarios.






TipInfographic and Code Guidance




Infographic: Most effective visualisations are usually a combination of images: maps + plots + icons, etc.
Code: Teams are strongly encouraged to submit any code developed during the Hackathon. This may include the creation of a GitHub repository to share scripts, data pipelines, or analysis workflows. Open and well-documented code will help ensure the solutions are reusable and scalable for future disaster response efforts.

🛠️ Note: UoL may implement the proposed indicators into a software package and is eager to take the lead in developing this package as part of a collaborative effort.


This Hackathon offers an opportunity to contribute to tools and strategies that could significantly improve disaster response and resilience, not just in Pakistan but globally.

title: Output
text:
Output to be submitted






ImportantPresentation and Code Submission



Each team should prepare a concise presentation (around 5 slides) to showcase their work at the end of the Hackathon. Focus on clear and compelling communication of your findings—impact matters as much as accuracy.
In addition, teams are strongly encouraged to submit any code developed during the Hackathon. Sharing your code (e.g., via a GitHub repository) helps ensure that your solutions are reproducible, scalable, and ready to support future disaster response efforts.

title: Digital Traces SitReps
text:
Colombia SitRep
A core output of FloodTraces is a set of Digital Traces Situation Reports — concise, policy-oriented documents that translate digital trace pipeline outputs into actionable displacement assessments for humanitarian decision-makers.
Each SitRep is designed to be:
Event: Chocó and north-easter floods, November 2024
Analysis window: October–November 2024
Validation benchmark: OCHA reported displaced population (~28,500 people)
The Colombia SitRep applies a difference-in-differences analytical design using GPS mobility data from Veraset. Treated units are admin2 areas within the flood-affected north-easter corridor; control units are comparable unflooded admin2 areas elsewhere in Colombia. Displacement is defined as an absence from the home location of three or more consecutive days, with sensitivity checks.
Key confounder events within the analysis window — including public holidays, agricultural cycles, and major population movements — are accounted for.
The Colombia SitRep will be presented on Day 1 of the policy workshop.

title: Digital Traces SitReps
text:
Indonesia SitRep
Event: Multi-province flooding, early 2024
Analysis window: February–March 2024
Validation benchmark: IOM DTM displacement figures
The Indonesia SitRep follows the same GPS pipeline and DiD framework as Colombia, adapted to Indonesia’s administrative structure and flood geography.
The Indonesia SitRep will be presented on Day 1 of the policy workshop.

title: Digital Traces SitReps
text:
Pakistan SitRep
Event: Monsoon floods, August–October 2022
Status: Hackathon case study — participant-produced SitRep
Pakistan’s 2022 floods were among the most severe climate disasters in recent history, affecting over 33 million people and displacing millions across Sindh, Balochistan, and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. The Pakistan case provides the analytical foundation for the hackathon — with the goal that participating teams produce a Digital Traces SitRep for Pakistan, adding a third country example to sit alongside Colombia and Indonesia.
Unlike the Colombia and Indonesia SitReps — which use the FloodTraces GPS pipeline — the Pakistan hackathon draws on Meta Data for Good mobility and population datasets alongside IOM DTM rounds 1–6 from the 2022 response. Teams will engage with questions of cross-source validation, signal comparability, and how digital trace findings can be communicated to operational audiences. A successful output would be a SitRep that follows the same structure as the Colombia and Indonesia reports, adapted to the data sources and humanitarian context of Pakistan.
Full data documentation will be circulated to hackathon participants at the pre-event meeting.

title: Digital Traces SitReps
text:
What a SitRep Contains
Each Digital Traces SitRep follows a common structure:







Section
Content




Event summary
Flood dates, affected geography, OCHA/IOM headline figures


Data sources
GPS provider, coverage statistics, known gaps


Displacement signal
Estimated displaced population by admin unit and time period


Validation
Comparison with OCHA/IOM benchmark; agreement and divergence


Limitations
Demographic biases, device penetration, confounder risks


Analytical notes
DiD design, displacement threshold, sensitivity checks









Note



A note on benchmarks
SitRep validation is always conducted against displaced figures reported by OCHA, IOM or alternative Digital Trace derived estimates — not the broader “affected” population count, which includes people whose property or livelihoods were impacted without displacement. These figures differ substantially and the distinction is analytically critical.

title: Data Access
text:
Access your Snowflake account
To access your Snowflake account follow the instructions on this link that will provide you with a temporary account and access to the data for the Hackathon.

title: Data Access
text:
Working in Snowflake
Once you have access to your Snowflake account, to set up your Snowflake Notebook, follow the following steps:

In the Home page, where you land when you log in, select + Create > Notebook > New Notebook





In the pop up window, select the following options, then hit Create





In the Databases tab, you’ll find all the datasets available to you inside the PAKISTAN_FLOODS_2022_DATA database. Note the Schemas align to the dataset categories shared on the Data page





Navigate to the top right Packages dropdown to install your favourite Python packages from the Snowflake Conda Channel for you to use





Your Snowflake Notebook is ready to go! Create Markdown, SQL or Python cells to support you on your mission!

title: Data Access
text:
Working locally
If you prefer to work locally, you can use this Jupyter Notebook that has the information needed for you to connect to your Snowflake account from your laptop.

title: References
text:
International Organization for Migration (IOM), Dec 30 2024. DTM Pakistan – Flood Response Community Needs Identification (CNI) | Round 6 – Balochistan and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Provinces (August 2024). IOM, Pakistan.
Iradukunda, R., Rowe, F., & Pietrostefani, E. (2025). Producing population-level estimates of internal displacement in Ukraine using GPS mobile phone data. arXiv preprint arXiv:2504.00003.
Otto, F. E., Zachariah, M., Saeed, F., Siddiqi, A., Kamil, S., Mushtaq, H., … & Clarke, B. (2023). Climate change increased extreme monsoon rainfall, flooding highly vulnerable communities in Pakistan. Environmental Research: Climate, 2(2), 025001.
Rowe, F. (2022). Using digital footprint data to monitor human mobility and support rapid humanitarian responses. Regional Studies, Regional Science, 9(1), 665-668.
Waleed, M., & Sajjad, M. (2025). High-resolution flood susceptibility mapping and exposure assessment in Pakistan: An integrated artificial intelligence, machine learning and geospatial framework. International Journal of Disaster Risk Reduction, 121(10544), 2.

title: FloodTraces
text:
Beyond Floods
While the project’s focus is flood-related displacement, the Digital Traces methodology is designed to transfer across disaster contexts. As a demonstration of this, we have applied the same GPS pipeline approach to conflict-induced displacement — producing a SitRep for Iran in early 2026:

Situation Report — Iran Conflict: Digital Trace Data Analysis (28 Feb – 19 Mar 2026)
Experimental reporting. Published on ReliefWeb.

This report illustrates how the same analytical approach — analysing population movement signals from digital trace data against a baseline period — can be adapted rapidly to new geographies and crisis types, with appropriate caveats on data coverage and representativeness.

title: Workshop
text:
Day 1 — Policy Workshop

10:00–11:00 — Welcome & SitRep Presentations
10:00–10:20 — Welcome & Project Framing (20 min)
Elisabetta Pietrostefani
Scene-setting: why floods, why digital trace data, and how Day 1 connects to the hackathon.
10:20–11:00 — Reading Floods: What New Data Can and Cannot Tell Us (40 min)

Digital trace data as a population signal — what the GPS pipeline produces, what it measures, and how to read it critically (10 min — Andrea Nasuto)
Indonesia & Colombia SitReps (20 min — Matt Mason)
Comparative discussion — what differs across contexts, what patterns hold (10 min — Martina Pardy)


11:00–11:30 — Coffee



11:30–13:00 — Digital Traces in Practice
Digital Traces in Practice: Possibilities and Limits (90 min)
A panel of flood and displacement specialists exploring what different data sources can and cannot tell us — and what should give policy audiences pause.

Francisco Rowe — Demographic skew and representativeness in digital trace data: rural/urban footprint, mobile penetration in flood-prone low-income areas, gendered device access (15 min)
Ana Varela Varela — Flood measurement and impact evaluation for a policy audience (15 min)
Zara Shabrina — Multidimensional datasets and flood trajectories: lessons from sinking Jakarta (15 min)
Fawad Qureshi — Validating flood policies through a structured lens using natural language processing (15 min)
Panel Q&A and synthesis (30 min)


13:00–14:00 — Networking Lunch



14:00–16:00 — Structured Feedback & Data Walkthrough
14:00–15:00 — Structured Feedback: Usefulness, Gaps & Adoption Pathways (60 min)
Facilitated session capturing on-the-record input from FCDO, IOM, NGOs, and partner organisations.
15:00–15:30 — Hackathon Data Walkthrough (30 min)
Live tour of the data packs: file structure, variable dictionaries, known limitations, and a notebook demo with working starter code.

15:30–16:00 — Coffee



16:00–17:30 — IOM Briefing & Team Kickoff
16:00–16:45 — IOM Operational Briefing: Pakistan & Colombia (45 min)
What DTM covers on the ground, what it misses, and what a genuinely useful hackathon output looks like from an operational standpoint.
16:45–17:30 — Team Kickoff Sprint
Scoping session. End-of-day deliverable: one-paragraph problem framing and a named team lead.

Evening — Networking Dinner (own cost; open to all attendees)

Evening — Closing Reception

title: Workshop
text:
Participation
Participation is structured around pre-assigned mixed teams. Each team includes at least one domain expert (humanitarian or policy), three technical participants (data or code), and one academic contributor. Effective mixing is deliberate — the strongest hackathon outputs combine operational knowledge with analytical capability.






Note



Pre-hackathon meetings
Two online pre-hackathon meetings will take place during the week of 15 June 2026. These sessions cover team introductions, problem statement confirmation, and a guided walkthrough of the data packs.

title: Outputs
text:
Meeting your team and agreeing on roles
Confirming your chosen problem statement
Walkthrough of the data packs and access via Zotera
Q&A with the organising team

title: Outputs
text:
Expected Outputs
Each team produces one of the following, depending on their chosen problem statement:
1. Pakistan Digital Traces SitRep (Replicate and Refine)
A new Situation Report for the 2022 Pakistan floods, following the same structure as the Colombia and Indonesia SitReps presented on Day 1. Teams should include analytical notes on methodology, data limitations, and validation against IOM DTM figures. The goal is a report that could sit alongside the existing SitReps as a third country example.
2. Interactive SitRep (Make It Interactive)
A storymap or interactive map that brings a Situation Report to life for a non-technical policy audience. Users should be able to explore displacement data dynamically — filtering by geography, time, or severity. The best outputs will be linked from future SitReps via QR code.
3. Anticipatory Action Analysis (See It Coming)
A piece of analysis or prototype tool exploring early warning signals, vulnerability mapping, or anticipatory indicators using historical Pakistan flood data. Outputs may take the form of a short analytical report, a notebook, or a dashboard — whatever best communicates the findings to an operational audience.

All outputs should be accompanied by a short presentation (5 slides maximum) for the Day 3 final session, and teams are strongly encouraged to share any code via GitHub repository.






Note



Recognition and publication
All team contributions incorporated into future published works will be acknowledged. Outputs will feed into a forthcoming policy paper on digital trace data for humanitarian action. Teams whose SitReps are refined to publication standard will be supported to publish them on ReliefWeb — as the Iran conflict SitRep was in early 2026.

title: FloodTraces
text:
Join Us
Day 1 of the policy workshop (22 June) is open to remote attendance. If you work in humanitarian response, policy, or displacement data and would like to join online, we welcome your participation.
To register interest or find out more, contact Elisabetta Pietrostefani at e.pietrostefani@liverpool.ac.uk.
The hackathon (Days 2–3) is for pre-registered participants only. If you are interested in future editions, please get in touch.
